mcqs.question: What special member function is automatically invoked when an object of a class goes out of scope?
mcqs.explanation: The destructor is called automatically when the object's lifetime ends.

mcqs.question: Which constructor is called when an object is created without any arguments?
mcqs.explanation: The default constructor is invoked when no arguments are supplied.

mcqs.question: In a class that contains a const data member, which mechanism must be used to initialize that member?
mcqs.explanation: Const members must be initialized using a member initializer list because they cannot be assigned after construction.

mcqs.question: If a derived class object is deleted via a pointer to its base class and the base class destructor is not declared virtual, what is the outcome?

options.text: Only the base class destructor runs (undefined behavior for derived part)

mcqs.explanation: Without a virtual destructor, the derived class destructor is not invoked, leading to resource leaks and undefined behavior.

mcqs.question: What is the primary purpose of the copy assignment operator?

options.text: To initialize a new object from another object
To assign the contents of one existing object to another of the same class
To free resources when an object is destroyed
To transfer ownership of resources from one object to another

mcqs.explanation: The copy assignment operator assigns values to an already existing object, replacing its contents.

mcqs.question: Given the inheritance hierarchy: class A {}; class C {}; class B : public A, public C {}; class D : public B {}. When a D object is destroyed, what is the order of destructor calls?
mcqs.explanation: Destructors run in reverse order of construction: D, then B, then C (the bases in reverse order of declaration), then A.

mcqs.question: Which statement about delegating constructors in C++11 is correct?

options.text: A constructor can delegate to another constructor and also have other member initializers in the same initializer list
A delegating constructor cannot have any other member initializers
Delegating constructors are not allowed to have a constructor body
The delegated-to constructor must be a base class constructor

mcqs.explanation: When a constructor delegates, its initializer list can only contain the delegation; no other members or bases can be initialized in that list.

mcqs.question: According to the rule of three (C++98) and rule of five (C++11), which set of special member functions must be either all defined or all defaulted to avoid resource leaks?

options.text: Destructor, copy constructor, copy assignment operator
Destructor, copy assignment operator, move assignment operator

mcqs.explanation: The rule of three specifies destructor, copy constructor, and copy assignment operator; the rule of five adds the two move members.
